title: Plato's Problem — the leap no citation would show you
subtitle: Follow concept-learning until a semantic wormhole leaps clean out of the field.

steps.title: Cognition from connections
steps.body: Connectionism: the idea that thinking emerges from networks of simple units, each doing almost nothing on its own. "Parallel Distributed Processing" is its banner — and we've parked on it, its details open, deep in computer science. We're going to follow a single thread out of here: how a network comes to KNOW things — to hold a concept, to mean something. It's a question that doesn't stay in computer science.

steps.title: The sky resolves into fields
steps.body: Switch on the field map: every paper resolves into its discipline — each field its own glowing galaxy. We're inside Computer Science. The other fields hang at a distance, with faint threads already crossing between them: connections leaving computer science for other disciplines entirely. Hold onto that — the idea we're chasing won't stay in this galaxy.

steps.title: Where does meaning come from?
steps.body: Follow a citation out of this paper. It crosses — already — into psychology: "Semantic Cognition," a study of how a network gradually comes to represent what things MEAN: what a robin is, how it differs from a rose. We've changed galaxies on an ordinary citation, and we're still chasing the same single idea — how a network comes to know. An idea isn't bounded by its field. Hold that thought.

steps.title: Acquiring a concept
steps.body: Keep following the thread. A 1991 model — "A modular neural network model of concept acquisition" — asks not just how a network REPRESENTS a concept, but how it LEARNS a new one at all. The citation carries us back into computer science. We've stayed inside a single idea the whole way: how a network comes to know. This is where it gets strange.

steps.title: Not every link is a citation
steps.body: Two violet conduits bloom from this node — semantic kin the citation graph never drew: papers that MEAN something similar, in other fields. One reaches another network model of how minds form categories — still inside machine learning. The other leaps clean out of it, into another galaxy entirely. We turn to face the far one.

steps.title: Plato's Problem
steps.body: The humanities. "A solution to Plato's problem" — latent semantic analysis, 1997. It answers a question Plato asked 2,400 years ago: how do we come to know so much from so little? And its answer is the very idea we've been following — meaning learned from patterns of co-occurrence, the same mechanism a neural network uses to acquire a concept. A model of mind, alive in philosophy — and no citation chain would ever have walked you here.
